notes.note: The letter-heading; the first of two consecutive recommendations (13.43, 13.44) addressed to Q. Gallius on behalf of L. Oppius and the absent banker L. Egnatius Rufus. The praenomen and nomen are uncertain: the manuscripts give "Quintius Gallus," usually normalized to Q. Gallius.

notes.anchor: L. Oppius, son of Marcus, does business at Philomelium
notes.note: L. Oppius was a negotiator (a Roman businessman, typically engaged in trade and money-lending) based at Philomelium, a town in Phrygia in Asia Minor. He is to be distinguished from C. Oppius, Julius Caesar's confidential agent.

notes.anchor: he also has charge of the affairs of L. Egnatius Rufus, the one Roman knight with whom I am most closely connected
notes.note: L. Egnatius Rufus was a banker and Roman knight with extensive provincial business interests; Cicero repeatedly commended his agents to provincial governors. Oppius here acts as his procurator (business manager) in his absence.

notes.anchor: I should be glad if, for memory's sake, you would write yourself something in a letter, to be delivered to you in your province
notes.note: A practical touch of the genre: Cicero asks Gallius to copy out a note for himself so that, once he reaches his province and the matter arises, the force of this commendation will be fresh in his mind.
